data.title: Imperial Petroleum: Ships Deliver, Shares Don't
data.description:
Elevator Thesis

Imperial Petroleum (IMPP) seems almost too cheap at first glance.

Imperial is a shipping company that operates product and crude oil tankers, as well as dry bulk carriers.

Picture this: profitable Q3, expanding fleet, increasing utilization, almost $172 million in cash, and no debt. It is hard to find such balance sheet strength in shipping.

The company is also operating successfully. The fleets have been on the go, and the cash generation is active.

So, why does it feel like something is off with the stock?

In essence, the investors do not have any concern about the fleet. The actual issue is what the management does with the value those ships create.

Imperial has been growing by issuing stock below the true value of the fleet. This definitely increases business, but it dilutes the shareholders by making every share worth less value. That kills confidence in the long term, and the market continues to have a low value on the stock.

The company is solid enough; however, discipline matters. This is where I believe that it is justifiable to hold and watch.

How Imperial Turns Ships Into Cash and Why Small Rate Changes Matter

The business of Imperial appears simple, with the conversion of ships to cash being the actual value.

Interestingly, profitability depends on three factors: the number of days the ships run, the daily rate the ships earn, and the difference between the revenue and the costs that are mostly fixed. The distinction is the free cash flow, and free cash flow is what determines the per-share value.

The engine is running hot, and Q3 2025 is a perfect example. With 7 new dry bulk ships fully integrated, revenue jumped 25% YOY, and utilization increased to 89% compared to 65% in the previous year. Around 75% of fleet days were on time-charter contracts, and this held cash flow steady despite choppiness in spot markets.

Additionally, operating income skyrocketed 72% to $10.3 million, and net income was maintained at $11 million, or $0.30 per share, even though there were more shares outstanding.

As IMPP's CEO Harry Vafias stated in the Q3 earnings call,

“Our strong operating cash flow generation supported our fleet expansion during the past couple of years.” The fleet is not just growing; it is producing real earnings.

Even small rate increases have a significant impact. A single vessel with an increase of $3,000 per day translates to about $1.1 million per year. Across 20 vessels, that would contribute about $21.9 million to revenue, the majority of which would be directly entered into EBITDA and FCF.

Moreover, the returns are excellent when one considers each ship. A 50k DWT product tanker purchased at a cost of $20.6 million will generate a revenue of $6-7 million and EBITDA of $3-4 million, a mid-teens payoff. In good market conditions, each handysize bulk carrier has the potential to make $2 million in addition to EBITDA. It can turn each ship into a concrete shareholder value.

Imperial also depends on deferred and interest-free acquisitions, which enable cash to work till the full purchase price is recouped. Yes, the fleet is cash-generating, but the biggest question is whether this strength can translate into the per-share gains. As charters continue to roll off and dilution is still on the cards, winning the operation alone is not good enough to ensure shareholders' benefits.

Ownership is Aligned, but Trust is Still on Trial

It is true that management alignment exists, but that is not everything.

On the positive side, Harry Vafias does have some real skin in the business. He has about 30% of TNK's common stock and has never sold a single share.

That matters. Dilution will harm him as much as it will harm outside shareholders. This implies that his incentives are long-term and not transactional. Notably, per-share value is also something the management has been considerate of in the past.

In 2023, the stock was heavily discounted, and Imperial repurchased 4.25 million shares and $10 million of the warrants, which in turn immediately boosted the NAV per share and reduced future dilution. Nevertheless, alignment does not necessarily imply discipline.

After the 2021 spin-off, Imperial has grown its fleet from 4 to 19 vessels, with many being funded through the recurring equity raises. In the same period, the number of shares increased rapidly compared to the fleet, and the trend has been consistent.

The most apparent one was in the previous month. Imperial issued $60 million of stock at $6.30 per share, increasing the number of shares in one go by approximately 30%. The management sold the stock at a huge discount and pegged the NAV at $11.38. That's actual value out of the pockets of existing shareholders, and the market caught it.

Meanwhile, there is the concern of governance optics. Imperial has also purchased vessels from a related party, Brave Maritime. Independent directors approved these deals, and third-party valuations supported them. Nonetheless, recurring insider purchases along with dilution weaken trust. Reasonable pricing is valuable, but perception of the market is also critical in the valuation.

The numbers speak volumes. For the first 9 months of 2025, EPS clocked in at $0.98 compared to $1.47 the previous year. Total earnings were supported by fleet growth, and every share earned less.

The problem now is the gap that management needs to resolve. The company is finally scaled, liquid, and debt-free. Credibility here is grounded on restraint. In case management pauses dilution, allows cash flow to compound, and focuses on NAV growth and EPS increase, the discount could close. Otherwise, Imperial will be stuck as a permanent discount stock where value is created but never reaches shareholders.

Valuation Analysis

IMPP is trading at roughly $3.8, way below its intrinsic value. The net asset value per share is approximately twice the stock price, even after the recent equity offering. This means that the market has not yet realized the operational capability of the fleet. This warning is not related to the vessels themselves, but it is based on past dilution and the purchase of related-party vessels.

The one-year price trend (above snapshot) gives a better picture. The stock was fairly stable in the first half of 2025. It began to climb in September as the fleet growth and high earnings were evident, reaching above $6 in November. But following the December equity raise at $6.30 per share, the price plummeted to approximately $3.8. This action is indicative of market skepticism.

Investors reacted more to dilution and capital allocation issues than to operational performance. The chart shows that even though the business is performing, the market is centred on per-share value instead of total earnings.

This disconnect is present in forward EPS estimates. Earnings are estimated at $1.29 in 2025 and $2.02 in 2026, yet forward P/E ratios are very low. It also points to the market pricing in doubt, but not the company's capacity to generate earnings. If the management is able to convert fleet productivity into per-share gains, it has a strong upside potential.

This view is supported by scenario-based discounted cash flow models. With a conservative assumption of a small rise in FCF, a WACC of 10%, and a terminal growth of 0.5%, the per-share value is $4.24.

A base case with moderate growth, a WACC of 9%, and a terminal growth rate of 1% produces a payout of $4.94 per share, which is in line with the gradual increase in the EPS.

The price history indicates that the market will only reward execution that maintains per-share value.

Risks to the Thesis

Imperial Petroleum is exposed to various risks that investors cannot overlook.

The shipping market is volatile and unpredictable. Despite the majority of vessel days being on time-charter contracts, a long-term decline in freight rates may squeeze the margins and reduce cash flow. Spot rates are quite unstable, and even minor changes in the global supply or demand can disproportionately impact revenue.

Another major risk is capital allocation. The company has a record of equity raises and related-party vessel acquisitions that have diluted shareholders on numerous occasions. While fleet expansion increases total earnings, per-share growth may fall behind as new shares are issued at severe discounts.

Unless the management focuses on scale over disciplined deployment of cash, the market will remain reserved and the stock will be undervalued despite operational strength.

There are also possibilities of operational disruptions. Temporary downtime and unnecessary expenses may affect outcomes since multiple vessels need maintenance next year.

Other external forces like geopolitical tensions, regulatory changes, and environmental regulations are also wildcards that can shape trade flows, shipping routes, and operating costs.

Takeaway

The plot is not merely to grow bigger. It is whether such growth is benefiting you as a shareholder. The management has been able to create value, but old decisions have diluted returns in some instances. The emphasis should now be on discipline, careful allocation, and making sure that each ship makes per-share contributions.

The most effective way is to hold and watch at the moment. Focus on performance and actual results rather than headlines. If the company shows the ability to grow without reducing shareholder value, that is when things become interesting. In the meantime, it is wiser to remain patient and quality-oriented rather than swayed by hype.

data.title: Oracle Is On A Roll But Debt Burden Fears Seem Valid
data.description:
Oracle Corporation (ORCL) is undergoing a major strategic shift where the company is focused on transforming from a software giant into an AI infrastructure solutions provider. The company is aggressively investing to support these efforts.

A closer look at its balance sheet reveals that long-term debt has been rising steadily since mid-2022. The first trigger was the acquisition of Cerner for $28.3 billion. This was an all-cash offer, which the company funded mostly through debt. Oracle has a history of covering shareholder distributions with debt as well. However, the most recent spike in debt has come on the back of aggressive AI infrastructure investments (including $18 billion raised last September). From $72 billion at the end of Fiscal 2022, Oracle's long-term debt has ballooned to almost $100 billion today.

In this analysis, I will be focusing on the current driver of long-term debt, aka AI infrastructure investments. As I discuss in this analysis, the prospects for the cloud business are bright. Early performance indicators suggest this business is off to a great start. However, on the flip side, aggressive investments in this business segment are likely to force Oracle to tap into capital markets to remain solvent, distribute dividends, and invest in growth.

Oracle's Business Transformation Is Proving To Be Costly

Oracle used to be a high-margin software business. But not anymore. The company has pivoted aggressively to build AI data centers. With the rise of AI, Founder and CTO Larry Ellison has sought to position the company as an infrastructure solutions provider that enables the aggressive deployment of AI technology. Oracle's new business strategy is to invest in building physical data centers to power AI databases across all cloud environments (Oracle is now following a multi-cloud strategy, not just focused on OCI).

Michael Burry, who shot to fame after correctly predicting the housing market bubble in 2008, is betting against Oracle Corporation, according to a recent Substack post. For those who have not been following Mr. Burry in recent times, he liquidated his hedge fund (Scion Asset Management) last November and pivoted to publishing his investment research on Substack. His bearish stance on Oracle primarily stems from the company's rising debt load.

According to co-CEO Clay Magouyrk, Oracle already operates 147 cloud regions across the globe (this includes public regions, dedicated regions, and multi-cloud regions). 64 new locations are expected to join this cloud network in the coming years, bringing the total count to 211. The public region count has expanded to 51 as well.

Exhibit 1: Existing and planned Oracle public cloud regions

To become an AI infrastructure giant, Oracle needs to invest in building data centers. This requires billions of dollars in new investments. This much is very clear. In addition to this, some of Oracle's strategic decisions are contributing to the spike in CapEx. For instance, as part of Oracle's multi-cloud strategy, the company has decided to strategically embrace third-party chips that are costly to acquire (these chips are bought from the likes of Nvidia and AMD). Historically, Oracle relied on customized chips developed by Ampere, a company in which Oracle held an ownership stake until the last quarter (Oracle sold its minority stake in Ampere and booked a gain of $2.7 billion). Moving away from Ampere-designed chips is proving to be costly, as expected. However, chip neutrality is important for Oracle's AI cloud infrastructure growth efforts. So this decision seems rational.

In addition to this, the multi-cloud strategy is likely driving CapEx higher as Oracle is embedding its hardware in data centers owned by the likes of AWS, GCP, and Azure to expand its reach.

The numbers reveal how costly this business transformation is.

Oracle was forced to raise its Fiscal 2026 CapEx forecast by $15 billion to $50 billion. This shows the sheer scale of investments required to build the data centers that are required to meet the contracted work (more on the backlog later). In Fiscal Q2 (the most recent quarter), Oracle reported a $10 billion free cash outflow despite operating income growth. This was primarily driven by the $12 billion in CapEx the company incurred during the quarter. For the 12 months ended on November 30, 2025, FCF was negative ~$13 billion. This was in complete contrast to the $9.5 billion in FCF Oracle reported for the 12 months ending on November 30, 2024. As you can see below, this decline in FCF came on the back of surging CapEx, not a deterioration in profitability or operating cash flows.

Exhibit 2: FCF comparison

There is no doubt that Oracle's business transformation is costing the company billions of dollars today. I think both Oracle bulls and Michael Burry can agree with this. For investors, the more important question is whether Oracle can realize handsome returns from these investments.

Early Revenue Growth Momentum Suggests Oracle Has Room To Grow

Oracle's business transformation strategy gets interesting here. Rather than building capacity and looking to fill that capacity with new orders, Oracle is doing the opposite. Today, the company is focused on securing new contracts and then building the required capacity to fulfill these contracts in the future. Oracle ended Fiscal Q2 with RPO of $523.3 billion. This was a fivefold increase compared to the corresponding quarter of the previous year. A closer look at the growth of this backlog (in Q2 alone, the backlog grew by almost $70 billion) reveals that Oracle has secured committed contracts. This is not the same as Oracle reporting a surge in its backlog because of non-committed purchasing intentions.

There are a few reasons to believe that the growth in RPO is high-quality.

For instance, on the Q2 earnings call, co-CEO Clay Magouyrk shed some light on the contract approval process and said:

We analyze land and power for data center buildings, component supply, including GPUs, network gear and optics, labor costs for all phases of construction and low voltage work, engineering capacity to design, build and operate, revenue and profitability and capital investments required. Only when all these components come together do we accept customer contracts, having the confidence we can deliver on schedule with the highest quality.

Oracle's backlog consists of contracts secured with several tech giants, including Meta Platforms, Inc. (META), Uber Technologies, Inc. (UBER), and even OpenAI. This is another indication of the health of this backlog.

Although the debt burden has increased substantially in recent years, Oracle's focus on converting CapEx into revenue de-risks its business transformation efforts. On the Q2 earnings call, the management highlighted how CapEx is expensed toward the end of the data center production cycle to minimize the time it takes to convert CapEx into revenue. This has been possible because of Oracle's strategy of securing contracts first, building infrastructure later.

I am not suggesting that Oracle's RPO growth is without any risks. Based on company filings, only 10% of total RPO is expected to be realized as revenue within the next 12 months. I prepared the below table based on the data in Oracle's Q2 filing.

Percentage of RPO expected to be realized as revenue Time period
10% 12 months
30% Months 13 to 36
35% Months 37 to 60
25% Beyond 60 months

Source: 10-Q

Data in the above table highlights that investors need to play the long game before enjoying the expected rewards of Oracle's business transformation. However, there is no denying that Oracle has started off nicely. In addition to the contract wins I discussed earlier, the company's recent financial performance confirms this.

In FQ2, cloud infrastructure revenue grew 66% YoY to $4.1 billion. This was explosive, to say the least. Within this, revenue related to GPUs grew 177%. Cloud database services revenue was up 30% YoY in FQ2 as well. Multicloud consumption (which the company has strategically prioritized in the recent past) was up 817% as well.

Early performance suggests Oracle's data center business is headed in the right direction. However, we should keep in mind that aggressive investments are needed to convert RPO into revenue. This is where challenges come to light. As I explain in the next segment of this analysis, investing in Oracle today is literally a bet on the expected success of its data center business.

Oracle Is Not As Healthy As It Seems From A Liquidity Perspective

Oracle has stood the test of time. Oracle ended the last quarter with almost $20 billion in cash. However, the ongoing business transformation has exposed the company to the possibility of a liquidity crunch. When I say a liquidity crunch, I don't mean to say that Oracle is facing bankruptcy risk. But the company may have to raise capital in the foreseeable future to meet its CapEx targets. To better explain this, I created the below table.

Category Financial statement item Amount Comments
Starting liquidity Cash and cash equivalents $19.8 billion
Projected cash inflows Operating cash flows for H2 FY 2026 $14 billion This is a very optimistic estimate vs H1 FY 2026 operating cash of $10.2 billion.
Projected cash outflows CapEx $29.5 billion Based on FY 2026 guidance for $50 billion and H1 FY 2026 reported CapEx of $20.5 billion.
Dividends $2.8 billion
Net cash flows Projected cash burn in H2 FY 2026 $18.3 billion Based on the above calculations.
Ending liquidity position Cash balance as of May 31, 2026 $1.5 billion
Immediate debt repayment obligations Debt maturing within 12 months $8.1 billion
The potential liquidity crunch Liquidity gap (cash-debt) $6.6 billion This represents a shortfall.

Source: Author's estimates and company filings

The $6.6 billion cash deficit assumes Oracle fully repaying debt due within the next 12 months. However, given the current liquidity scenario, Oracle is more likely to refinance this debt. The company, therefore, is highly unlikely to face a liquidity crunch, but the point is that Oracle will be forced to tap into capital markets to remain solvent.

What this also means is that investors betting on Oracle stock today are effectively betting on its data center business delivering the promised goods in the long run.

Takeaway

Oracle is carrying almost $100 billion in long-term debt on its balance sheet. Since the Cerner acquisition, the company's debt load has risen sharply. Today, the main driver of debt is the company's aggressive business transformation strategy. Although the data center infrastructure business is showing promising signs, investors need to be careful of the growing debt burden.

data.title: AMZA: Compelling Valuation, Remains A Buy
data.description:
It has been a volatile beginning to 2026 for oil & gas shares. Even domestic midstream master limited partnership units have struggled amid low oil prices and suddenly sagging natural gas. I had a buy rating on the InfraCap MLP ETF (AMZA) almost a year ago. Shares have lost 9% on a total return basis, underperforming the S&P 500 by more than 20 percentage points.

Today, the 8.6% dividend-yielding ETF sports a lower valuation but a concerning chart. For now, I’ll keep with a buy rating, but there is one key technical price point that bears watching this year.

AMZA: Weak YoY Performance With Low Natural Gas Prices

According to the issuer, AMZA seeks to provide exposure to midstream master limited partnerships (MLPs) with an emphasis on high current income. The ETF employs modest leverage (typically 20-30%) to target strong total return results, and security selection and weightings are based on security-level fundamental analysis and technical factors instead of market capitalization. It invests in 25-35 midstream MLPs, including publicly traded limited partnerships and limited liability companies taxed as partnerships, as well as related general partners.

AMZA remains a small ETF, now with just $386 million in assets under management as of January 9, 2026. That’s down from $454 million at the time of my previous analysis. The annual expense ratio is listed as a very high 2.75%, but like a closed-end fund that employs leverage, the figure includes the cost of debt related to gaining added MLP exposure. Hence, the dividend yield is very high at close to 9% today.

AMZA rates rather poorly on its ETF Risk Grade, according to Seeking Alpha’s quantitative scoring system. A concentrated allocation and an elevated 19.2% historical standard deviation percentage make this by no means a pure-yield play—the net asset value and ETF share price can swing around. So, acknowledging that going in is critical.

Share-price momentum, meanwhile, is a soft C+. That is even worse, considering that the global stock market is at an all-time high. Geopolitical regime changes affecting the oil & gas markets have so far only added to soft price action too.

Moreover, liquidity is not ideal with the fund—average daily volume is only about 35,000 shares, while the median 30-day bid/ask spread is wide at 58 basis points, per Virtus Investment Partners. Thus, I encourage prospective investors to use limit orders, particularly around the market open.

For an update on the portfolio, the 2-star, Neutral-rated ETF by Morningstar is primarily a SMID-cap value product. More than half of the allocation is classified as mid-cap value, while just 2% of the fund is growth. You’ll see that leverage is not extreme—only about 20%. AMZA does not have any international exposure, either, so it has not benefited from the US dollar’s drop in value.

Today, AMZA sports a low 11.6x price-to-earnings ratio, while the long-term earnings growth rate is actually rather decent at 9.6%. The resulting PEG ratio is compelling at 1.25x.

AMZA: Portfolio & Factor Profiles

AMZA is a very concentrated fund. The top 10 holdings account for 97% of the equity exposure. So, investors could replicate much of the product on their own, but that would ignore the leverage employed by the fund managers. But with the Q4 earnings season on the horizon, monitoring both the fundamentals and technicals with AMZA is key.

AMZA: Holdings & Dividend Information

Seasonally, AMZA tends to do well in January, but bearish price action has been the trend over the past 10 years in February and March.

AMZA: Weaker February-March Calendar Trends

The Technical Take

With an improved valuation compared to early 2025, AMZA’s technical situation is precarious. Notice in the chart below that a bearish rounded top formation has developed, with critical support between $37 and $38.

So long as the ETF is above that range, a long position can make sense technically. A drop below it, however, and a bearish downside measured move price objective to near $26 would be in play based on the height of the consolidation pattern. You may notice that it resembles a head-and-shoulders topping pattern, too.

Also take a look at the RSI momentum oscillator at the top of the graph. It has been in a bearish range between 20 and 60 for the better part of the last year. That, along with a falling long-term 200-day moving average, suggests that the bears control the primary trend.

Finally, resistance is seen just below the $42 mark, along with a high amount of volume by price from $39 to $45, so we could see shares trade in the current range for quite a while.

AMZA: Bearish Head and Shoulders Rounded Topping Pattern

The Bottom Line

I have a buy rating on AMZA. The fund of MLP units has lagged the global stock market in the past year, but the valuation has improved. Low natural gas prices have been a reason for the poor return, and the chart is concerning as we progress through 2026.

data.title: Franklin Small Cap Growth SMA Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Key Takeaways

  • Markets: US equities delivered strong performance over the third quarter of 2025, buoyed, in part, by easing trade tensions and optimism surrounding artificial intelligence (AI). The US Federal Reserve’s (Fed’s) interest-rate cut in September also supported market sentiment, as did generally robust corporate earnings. Both the S&P 500 Index and the Nasdaq Composite Index ended the quarter at new record highs, while returns for the Dow Jones Industrial Average were more muted. In this environment, growth investing surpassed value in the large-capitalization tier, while it was the reverse in the mid- and small-capitalization segments, with small-cap stocks overall performing better than their large- and mid-cap counterparts.
  • Detractors: Both stock selection and allocation effects were drags on the strategy’s overall relative performance. Stock selection in the information technology (IT) sector was the largest source of negative relative returns, followed by stock selection and an overweight in the consumer staples sector and stock selection in the consumer discretionary sector.
  • Contributors: The bulk of the strategy’s relative strength came from stock selection in the financials and health care sectors, which benefited from investments in the capital markets and biotechnology industries, respectively.
  • Outlook: The performance of small-cap stocks in general has been positive so far this year, supported by valuations that we consider to be favorable, as well as by earnings growth and monetary policy. In our view, future performance will likely depend on the health of the US economy, along with Fed decisions and overall investor confidence. We believe small-cap growth stocks are attractively valued, both relative to large caps and in light of their underlying growth potential.

Performance Review

  • The strategy’s return (pure gross of fees) was positive but lagged that of the Russell 2000 Growth Index. The strategy also underperformed the index on a net-of-fee basis for the period. Hindering relative returns was monday.com in the IT sector. Shares of the cloud-based collaborative platform provider fell due to a weak outlook, despite strong second-quarter financial results.
  • In the consumer staples sector, shares of BellRing Brands (BRBR) declined. Retailer inventory adjustments and reduced full-year 2025 guidance were headwinds for the nutrition products company.
  • Benefiting relative returns was our holding in Merus in the health care sector. There were several catalysts for its stock’s outperformance, including the company’s promising clinical trial results for a head and neck cancer treatment and an announcement of its takeover by a Danish biotechnology company.

Outlook

  • While headlines have focused on trade tensions, elevated interest rates and fiscal uncertainty, both historical precedent and current market performance reinforce our core thesis that the enduring strength of US businesses and capital markets should help drive global economic growth into the next decade. We believe US companies and the broader economy continue to be powered by structural advantages and secular growth drivers.
  • In broad terms, US companies have pioneered breakthroughs that have reshaped industries—ranging from the internet and semiconductors to genomics and defense technologies. In our view, US companies are positioned at the forefront of generative artificial intelligence, semiconductors, biotechnology and space exploration, creating powerful long-term tailwinds for investors seeking exposure to the world’s leading growth companies.
  • We see powerful secular tailwinds that potentially support US market leadership in the years ahead. Generative AI and automation can unlock productivity and cost savings across sectors. Pro-business policies—such as infrastructure investment, deregulation and tax incentives—continue to foster domestic growth. Onshoring and supply-chain diversification are reviving domestic manufacturing and boosting output, particularly in the advanced industrial and energy markets.
  • In our view, economic conditions remain constructive for risk assets, with resilient growth, moderate inflation and broadly supportive monetary and fiscal policy. Corporate fundamentals also appear healthy, in our view, amid improved earnings revisions. Our strategy maintains a diversified approach designed to help us participate in ongoing market strength while remaining adaptable to shifts in market leadership.
